Ayah 1–3 · linked video: 1. An-Nasr

PRIMARY_AYAH: 1

Surah An-Nasr: When Victory Comes

Introduction to the Surah

Surah An-Nasr is one of the briefest chapters in the Quran, containing only three ayahs. Despite its brevity, it carries profound significance as it marks a pivotal moment in Islamic history and in the life of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. The surah exists in two possible translations regarding its tense: some scholars interpret it in the past tense (“when the help of Allah and the victory arrived”), while others read it in the future tense (“when the help of Allah and the victory arrives”). This grammatical nuance opens different layers of meaning that we’ll explore.

The question of when this surah was revealed has been debated extensively among scholars of Tafsir. The majority view holds that it was revealed in Madinah, with some suggesting it came on the day of Makkah’s conquest, others saying it was revealed on the journey there, and still others placing it during the Prophet’s ﷺ residence in Madinah. However, there exists a compelling argument for this being a Makkan surah, which we’ll examine through its thematic connections to other parts of the Quran.

A famous narration illuminates the deeper meaning of this surah: Ibn Abbas (RA) once questioned the companions about its interpretation. After hearing various responses, he revealed that this was the farewell address of the Prophet ﷺ - Allah’s ﷻ way of telling His messenger that his time in this world was coming to an end. When this understanding emerged, it became clear why the surah came at the very end of the prophetic mission.

Ayah 1

إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ

“When the help of Allah and the victory arrives.”

The Meaning of Nasr (Divine Aid)

The word نصر carries deeper implications than simple help or assistance. In classical Arabic, this term originally referred to the pathways through which water flows from the mountaintops down into valleys. This imagery is crucial to understanding the concept: just as water travels a great distance and gathers into a reservoir that sustains life, divine aid comes from above as a life-giving force that arrives precisely when needed.

The Arabs used the word nasr for heavy rain - the kind of rain that truly helps the earth, enabling it to produce vegetation and fulfill its purpose. This wasn’t just any rain, but rain that came at the critical moment to revive dying land. Similarly, nasr in the Quranic context means援助 that arrives just in time, like rescue on the verge of death. It represents a great amount of help - the kind that makes the difference between life and death, survival and annihilation.

This is why nasr differs from other Arabic words for help like مساعدة or معونة. Each has its own nuance, but nasr specifically carries this sense of divine intervention arriving at the crucial moment, transforming a desperate situation into victory.

The Meaning of Fath (Opening/Victory)

The word الفتح operates on both literal and figurative levels. Literally, it means “opening” - like when the skies open up (فَتَحْنَا أَبْوَابَ السَّمَاءِ). Figuratively, it represents the opening of floodgates, the arrival of blessings, or the removal of difficulties. In the ancient world, when a city had been conquered, people would say “the city has been opened” because ancient cities had protective gates that, when breached, signified conquest. This imagery connects the concept of opening directly to victory.

Interestingly, the Quran also uses fath to mean “verdict” or “judgment,” as in يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ (“the Day of Opening/Judgment”). The underlying concept involves separation and distinction - separating truth from falsehood, distinguishing the believers from the disbelievers. This meaning becomes especially relevant in the Makkan context, where multiple religions competed for supremacy at the House of Allah ﷻ built by Ibrahim (AS). The fath would finally clarify which religion was to be followed at the sacred sanctuary.

The Sequence: Why Nasr Before Fath?

The sequence in this ayah is significant: nasr comes first, then fath. This follows natural logic - first you receive help, then as a result of that help, you achieve victory. The means precedes the end. However, this sequence contrasts with another surah that uses similar language. In Surah Al-Fath, Allah ﷻ says: إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُبِينًا - “Indeed, We have given you a clear victory,” where fath is mentioned before nasr. This variation requires us to understand the relationship between these two concepts in different contexts.

Both surahs appear at the openings of their respective chapters, and both discuss victory - one as a promise yet to be fulfilled, the other as already arrived. Understanding this correlation is essential to grasping the full message of Surah An-Nasr.

The Philosophy of Victory in the Quran

The Quranic concept of victory is not immediately obvious and represents one of the more subtle subjects in Allah’s ﷻ book. Even the companions themselves needed revelation to fully understand what constitutes true victory. The key to this understanding lies in the relationship between Surah An-Nasr and Surah Al-Fath, as both use the same vocabulary of nasr and fath.

To properly understand this ayah, we must examine a crucial moment in Islamic history: the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. This event fundamentally redefined how Muslims understand victory.

The Context: The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah

The story begins about a year after the Battle of the Trench (Al-Ahzab), when the Quraysh and allied tribes had surrounded Madinah, intent on annihilating the entire Muslim community. This wasn’t a battle on a distant field where only combatants faced each other - this was an invasion of the city itself, where civilians would have been slaughtered alongside fighters. The Muslims were saved only by the trench they had dug and by Allah’s ﷻ intervention through fierce winds that disrupted the enemy camp.

Less than a year after this near-catastrophe, the Prophet ﷺ saw a dream that the Muslims would perform Hajj. Acting on this vision, he gathered between 1,400 and 1,800 companions to make the pilgrimage. Crucially, they would go unarmed except for the small daggers needed to slaughter sacrificial animals. They would also take these animals with them, which would significantly slow their travel.

When news reached the Quraysh that the Muslims were approaching Makkah, their response was immediate: “This is a golden opportunity - let’s kill them on the way.” The Muslims, to avoid confrontation, took an alternate route so treacherous that the companions’ feet were bloodied and their shoes destroyed by jagged rocks. They arrived at a place called Hudaybiyyah, named after a hunched tree and a dried well. There, the Prophet’s ﷺ camel sat down and refused to move. The companions tried to make it continue, but the Prophet ﷺ said, “We’re camping here.”

The well was dry, but after the Prophet ﷺ placed some of his blessed saliva in it and inserted an arrow, it overflowed with enough water for everyone and all the animals. The Muslims were now extremely close to Makkah - if you know New York geography, as close as Long Island is to Queens. The Quraysh panicked at this proximity.

The Assassination Attempt and the Rising Tensions

Unable to openly attack pilgrims (which would violate their own sacred customs), the Quraysh sent 80 cloaked assassins to kill the Muslims secretly at Hudaybiyyah. Remarkably, the Muslims - who were not well-armed - managed to disarm and capture all 80 assassins, unmasking them as Quraysh agents. Then, in an extraordinary display of restraint, they sent them back.

By this point, the Muslims who had scraped their feet raw to reach this place, who knew the Quraysh had come all the way to Madinah to destroy them, and who now saw that the enemy was willing to violate the sanctity of holy land to kill their Prophet ﷺ - these Muslims were ready for battle. Their blood was boiling with righteous anger.

The Prophet ﷺ conducted what can only be described as a psychological test: “What do you think we should do? Should we fight them?” The Muslims responded with unanimous resolve - they were ready to die, unafraid, seeking justice for the crimes committed against them. In the middle of this heated crowd, Abu Bakr (RA) raised his voice: “We didn’t come here for war. You saw a dream, O Messenger of Allah ﷺ - that’s what we came for.” The entire crowd began to calm down.

Failed Negotiations and Rising Provocations

The Quraysh, now exposed and unable to launch another attack, began negotiations. However, they were too proud to enter talks directly, so they sent representatives from neighboring tribes. These envoys tried various tactics: “They’re going to kill you. Look at them - you have people from Abyssinia, Persia, different tribes. What unity do you really have? They’re putting on their war gear, their lion cloaks. You need to leave.”

One negotiation attempt after another failed. In some cases, the Prophet ﷺ was openly insulted by Quraysh leaders. When this happened, Abu Bakr (RA), normally the voice of reason who had calmed everyone earlier, became enraged and cursed the insulter with language so foul that scholars recording the incident couldn’t even translate it fully. This demonstrates how the emotional temperature of the entire Muslim camp had shifted dramatically.

The Heartbreaking Case of Abu Jandal

The negotiations reached a breaking point with the arrival of Abu Jandal (RA). He was the son of Suhayl ibn Amr, the Quraysh negotiator who had fought against Muslims at Badr. At that battle, Suhayl had been captured and tied up by his own son who had embraced Islam. After being ransomed back to Makkah, Suhayl had taken his anger out on his other son, Abu Jandal, who had also become Muslim. He imprisoned Abu Jandal in a dungeon, keeping him in chains and beating him continuously.

When Abu Jandal heard that Muslims were nearby, he somehow escaped and came running to them, bloodied and still in chains. His father saw him and said, “The negotiation is off unless I can take him back.” The Prophet ﷺ refused to budge from the terms being discussed. Abu Bakr (RA) pleaded on Abu Jandal’s behalf, but the Prophet ﷺ held firm.

The terms of Hudaybiyyah were deeply unfavorable to the Muslims: Any Muslim who escaped from Makkah to Madinah would be returned. Any Muslim who left Islam and went to Makkah could stay. The Muslims could not perform Hajj that year despite having traveled all this way with their sacrificial animals. They would have to return home and could come back the following year.

The Muslims were furious about these terms, and now they were watching their own tortured brother being ordered to return to his abusers. The outrage was complete.

The Pledge of Ridwan

During these negotiations, Uthman ibn Affan (RA) had gone into Makkah to talk with the Quraysh and hadn’t returned all day. As hours passed, rumors began to spread: “Where’s Uthman? Maybe something happened to him. Maybe he was killed.” What started as speculation by a few people echoed through the camp until it became accepted as fact: Uthman has been killed.

Even the Prophet ﷺ became convinced of this news. The same man who had been keeping everyone calm now said, “Give me a pledge to the death. We’re going to avenge Uthman.” This pledge is recorded in the Quran as the Pledge of Ridwan (Bay’at al-Ridwan). The Prophet ﷺ himself took this pledge from his companions.

Understand what this meant: they were ready to attack Makkah itself, not a distant battlefield. They would enter the lion’s den with minimal weapons and face certain slaughter. Yet they were prepared to do it. As they readied themselves, Uthman (RA) walked back into camp, saying, “Sorry, it just took longer than expected.” The companions were so fired up with nowhere to direct their energy that they actually became upset with Uthman, complaining that he had gotten to see the Kaaba while they couldn’t. He defended himself, saying he wouldn’t have gone near the Kaaba without the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

The Command That Wasn’t Obeyed

After all these negotiations concluded with Abu Jandal being sent back, the Prophet ﷺ told everyone: “We’re going back. We’re not making Hajj. Shave your heads, slaughter the animals.” Here’s where it gets remarkable: the meat from these animals wouldn’t go back with them to Madinah. It would go to the Makkans who had just prevented them from performing Hajj, who had tried to kill their Prophet ﷺ, who had fought them in multiple wars including one less than a year ago where they tried to annihilate them at their doorstep.

For the first time in Islamic history, the Prophet ﷺ gave the companions a direct instruction and not one of them obeyed. This group, known for their instant response of “We hear and we obey,” simply stood there. The Prophet ﷺ repeated himself. Still nothing. No one moved.

The Prophet ﷺ went back to his tent, clearly troubled. It was Umm Salamah (RA) who advised him: “Go out yourself and do it. Don’t speak to anyone. Just shave your head and slaughter your animal. They’ll follow.” When the Prophet ﷺ did this, the companions begrudgingly followed suit. They slaughtered their animals with such passive-aggressive energy that when they killed the camel that had once belonged to Abu Jahl (which they’d captured at Badr), they placed its head on a giant platter and sent it first - a pointed reminder of their past victory.

Umar’s Crisis and the Revelation

As they headed back to Madinah, everyone remained deeply upset. Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA) felt especially troubled because he had raised his voice to the Prophet ﷺ - something the Quran warns will nullify all good deeds. He had questioned: “Aren’t you the true Prophet? Aren’t we the people of truth? Isn’t their religion false? Didn’t you say we would make Hajj this year? Why are we accepting their terms like beggars?”

The Prophet ﷺ had responded: “I never said this year.” But that answer wasn’t entirely satisfying - if not this year, then why did we come this year? Umar (RA) went to Abu Bakr (RA) with the same questions. Abu Bakr told him sternly: “You better watch yourself.” But even Abu Bakr (RA) hadn’t shaved his head initially.

As they traveled back, Umar (RA) rode up to the Prophet ﷺ three separate times trying to apologize, and three times the Prophet ﷺ didn’t respond. By the third attempt, Umar (RA) told himself, “Let my mother know her son is dead, because I’m doomed.”

Then someone came and called for Umar (RA). He approached the Prophet ﷺ terrified, thinking revelation had come about his transgression. Instead, he saw the Prophet’s ﷺ face brighter than the sun, filled with joy. Understand the context: nothing that had happened gave anyone reason for joy. The companions had behaved in a way they never had before. If there was ever a moment for the Prophet ﷺ to feel disappointed or disillusioned, this was it.

Yet the Prophet ﷺ was more joyful than Umar (RA) had ever seen him. He pulled Umar close and recited the entire Surah Al-Fath - all four pages of it. The surah begins: إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُبِينًا - “Indeed, We have given you a clear, manifest victory.”

When he finished reciting, Umar (RA), his head still spinning from processing this divine revelation, asked: “This was victory? This was an opening?” The Prophet ﷺ responded with the emphatic: “نَعَمْ” (Yes, indeed it is).

Umar (RA) immediately became the happiest of all the companions. Why? Because if the Prophet ﷺ says it’s victory, then it is victory. If a political scientist analyzes it, they’d say no. If any companion who hadn’t heard the revelation examines it, they’d say no. But if you ask Allah ﷻ through His word, He declares it is victory - not just victory, but فَتْحًا مُبِينًا - a clear, manifest, crystal-clear victory.

Redefining Victory

This moment fundamentally redefined how Muslims understand victory. Allah ﷻ did not validate victory as conquering on the battlefield, acquiring territory, or defeating enemies militarily. None of that happened at Hudaybiyyah. The true victory was something far more profound.

The victory was that under the most extreme circumstances - when these companions had every legitimate reason to fight, when their blood was boiling with righteous anger, when they had been insulted, attacked, denied their sacred right to worship, and watched their tortured brother dragged back to his abusers - they maintained discipline. The discipline they showed was nearly impossible for such a large group to maintain. The only shortage of discipline was a delay in shaving their heads and two companions having angry outbursts. That’s it.

Compare this to any other scenario: if you gathered 1,400 to 1,800 young and old men, showed them their enemy who had tried to kill them and insulted everything they held dear, could you even calm them enough to speak to them? Or would they riot? How would you do crowd control without modern technology, without microphones, without any way to hold them back?

The Prophet ﷺ stood before over 1,000 people with no amplification, and they stood listening. The fact that these companions could set aside their personal anger, their sense of justice, and their grievances to listen to the Prophet ﷺ - even when his commands made no sense to them - represented the greatest victory ever awarded to him. This discipline, this training, this maturity of faith qualified them for the divine aid that would follow.

We find confirmation of this in the Quran itself. The opening of Surah Al-Fath is explained by its conclusion, which describes the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and those with him as كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْأَهُ - “like a crop that puts forth its shoots then strengthens them.” If there was ever a time someone could say they weren’t “with him,” it was that moment at Hudaybiyyah. Yet Allah ﷻ confirms: they were absolutely with him. That is incredible. What they did on that occasion was amazing. But to Allah ﷻ - the One who sees everything - this wasn’t just amazing compared to human standards. This was the true, fundamental meaning of victory.

The Political Dimension

There’s also a remarkable political dimension to Hudaybiyyah that becomes clear only in hindsight. Imam Al-Razi and other scholars note that while the immediate circumstances seemed unfavorable, the treaty actually tied the hands of the Quraysh politically. They could no longer form alliances against the Prophet ﷺ as they had done before.

All the neutral tribes in the region witnessed something extraordinary: these Muslims had taken on the Quraysh multiple times. The Quraysh had come all the way to Madinah to destroy them and failed. Then the Muslims had the courage to come unarmed to Makkah’s doorstep, and the Quraysh - rather than fighting - negotiated with them. If a neutral observer was analyzing who was winning this political battle, the Muslims appeared increasingly powerful while the Quraysh’s influence was clearly shrinking.

During the treaty period, people and tribes began coming to investigate Islam. When a tribal leader accepted Islam, often his entire tribe would follow. Other tribes declared themselves neutral, refusing to ally with the Quraysh against the Muslims. The Quraysh had to abide by the treaty they themselves had signed.

Interestingly, the treaty stated that any man escaping from Makkah had to be returned, but it said nothing about women. When women would escape to Madinah, they could stay. The Muslims abided by the treaty’s letter. When the Quraysh eventually broke the treaty, this opened the door for what Allah ﷻ calls النَّصْرُ الْعَزِيزُ in Surah Al-Fath - the mighty, authoritative aid.

The word عَزِيز means not just mighty, but also dignified and authoritative. The Quraysh had spent years humiliating the Muslims. Now the tables had turned.

The March to Makkah

When the time came to fulfill this ultimate promise, the Prophet ﷺ marched toward Makkah with an army of 10,000. He ordered that when they reached a certain point, everyone should light a campfire. Imagine 10,000 fires illuminating the desert night on the outskirts of Makkah - an epic, overwhelming display of power. People in Makkah looking out at night saw this sight and wondered in awe and terror: “What is that?”

Al-Abbas (RA), the Prophet’s ﷺ uncle, was with the Muslim army. He said to himself, “What a morning is about to hit the Quraysh! If the Prophet ﷺ enters Makkah aggressively before they come to him seeking his protection, they will be annihilated. They’ll never recover.” Abbas was worried not about Muslims losing, but about all of the Quraysh dying.

He borrowed the Prophet’s ﷺ mule and rode into Makkah, hoping to meet someone and deliver a message: “We’re here. We’re about to take over. Go seek protection from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, apologize, say you mean no harm, and you’ll be fine.”

Some Quraysh leaders had come out to the city’s borders to see what was happening. When they saw the fires, they were shocked: “I’ve never seen fires like that. I’ve never seen an army that big.” One suggested, “This must be the tribe of Khuza’ah - they’re known for warfare.” Another responded, “No, Khuza’ah is much smaller and less powerful. This can’t be them.”

Then Abbas (RA) heard their voices and recognized them. He called out: “Abu Handhala!” (calling Abu Sufyan by his nickname). Abu Sufyan responded: “Is that you?” Abbas said, “Yes, it’s me.” Abu Sufyan replied with an expression of deep affection using the Arabic phrase that literally means “may my mother and father be sacrificed for you” - essentially, “I’ve missed you so much, I have so much love for you.”

Abbas (RA) said to him: “Listen - woe to you! You don’t know what’s coming. This is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ, and surrounding him are people from multiple tribes. What a morning is about to hit the Quraysh!”

Abu Sufyan - this once-powerful leader - asked desperately: “What’s the way out?”

Abbas replied: “If the Prophet ﷺ gets his hands on you now, he will cut your neck off. Get on the back of my mule. It belongs to the Prophet ﷺ, so it’s safe - like a presidential motorcade. I’ll take you all the way to him, and you can ask for his protection. That’s the only way for you, my friend.”

Abu Sufyan climbed onto the back of the mule while his two companions returned to Makkah to warn everyone. As Abbas slowly led the mule through the Muslim camp, they passed fire after fire. Each time they passed a group, the Muslims would ask, “Who is this?” These were the same words the Quraysh used to say dismissively about Muslims. Now the Muslims were asking “Who is this?” about the leader of the Quraysh. The equation had completely flipped.

When the Muslims recognized the Prophet’s ﷺ mule and saw Abbas (RA) on it, they said, “Oh, the Prophet’s ﷺ uncle is on the mule,” and let them pass. But when they reached the fire of Umar ibn Al-Khattab (RA), he stood up and walked over. He recognized Abu Sufyan on the back and exclaimed: “The enemy of Allah! I finally have the opportunity to get to you, and there’s no treaty or contract protecting you now!”

Umar (RA) wanted to kill him but restrained himself, saying, “I can’t do this without asking the Prophet ﷺ.” He rushed ahead to the Prophet’s ﷺ tent. Meanwhile, Abbas - who had promised Abu Sufyan safety - also hurried ahead.

Umar (RA) arrived first and said: “O Messenger of Allah, Allah ﷻ has given us the opportunity! There’s no contract, no treaty. Could you give me permission? I want to take his head off!”

Abbas countered: “I promised him safety!”

These were men who once had no voice in Makkah - Abbas and Umar (RA) - people who had been pushed around, kicked, expelled from their homes by the likes of Abu Sufyan. Now Abu Sufyan sat there listening to them negotiate whether to spare his life or take his head off. The reversal was complete.

Umar (RA) pressed his case, but Abbas challenged him: “If he was from your tribe, you wouldn’t want to kill him. You only want to kill him because he’s not from your tribe.”

Umar’s (RA) response was profound: “I swear to you, the day you became Muslim was more dear to me than if my own father had become Muslim. I acknowledge that your Islam is more beloved to the Prophet ﷺ than even my own father’s Islam would have been. So don’t tell me I’m being tribal.” This was Umar’s dignified way of defending himself while still respecting Abbas’s age and his relationship to the Prophet ﷺ as his uncle.

The Prophet ﷺ said: “Take him to your camp. We’re busy right now. We’ll deal with him in the morning.”

The Transformation of Abu Sufyan

This is how you treat a formerly powerful leader: put him on the back of a mule, discuss whether to kill him, then say, “I’ll deal with him in the morning.”

When morning came, the Prophet ﷺ said to Abu Sufyan: “Isn’t it time yet that you should come to know that there is no one worthy of worship except Allah?”

Abu Sufyan’s response revealed his character: “I would sacrifice my mother and father for you! How forbearing, how loving, how kind, how dignified you are! How mindful you are of family relationships!” He was appealing to his connection with the Prophet ﷺ through family ties, then added: “I swear to God, if I thought there was any other god besides Allah, that god would have helped me by now. Looking at my situation, there is only the God you believe in.”

The Prophet ﷺ continued: “Isn’t it time for you yet to acknowledge that I am Allah’s messenger?”

Abu Sufyan repeated his praise of the Prophet’s ﷺ character, then said: “As for this matter - that you’re the Messenger of Allah - I swear there’s still some doubt left in me about that.”

Abbas (RA) - his friend who had brought him safely here - said sharply: “Stop being foolish! Accept Islam before we take your head off!”

This intervention from his friend, who genuinely cared about him and was frustrated by his stubbornness, was what Abu Sufyan needed. He finally took the shahada and accepted Islam.

The Prophet ﷺ, recognizing that Abu Sufyan was someone who valued dignity and position, wanted to give him something that might help create a peaceful transition in Makkah. He said: “Whoever enters Abu Sufyan’s house is safe. Whoever closes their door is safe - we won’t break it down. Whoever enters the masjid is safe.”

Before Abu Sufyan left, Abbas (RA) suggested: “This man appreciates power and has valued it his whole life. Why don’t you make him stand at the narrow part of the valley where he can see all of Allah’s ﷻ armies pass by? I want him to see what it feels like to be powerless when power belongs only in Allah’s ﷻ hands.”

The Prophet ﷺ agreed. In the gentlest way, he was breaking Abu Sufyan’s pride and humbling him before sending him back.

As the armies passed, Abu Sufyan kept asking: “Who is this tribe?” Abbas would identify them. “I’ve never met them! What have I ever done to Salim?” These were people Abu Sufyan had never even heard of, yet they had come to fight on behalf of the Prophet ﷺ. Each new flag, each new tribe - he didn’t know them.

Finally, the Prophet’s ﷺ elite guard passed by under the green banner. These were his closest companions, completely covered in armor except for the black metallic shine. You couldn’t even see their eyes.

Abu Sufyan asked: “Who are these?”

Abbas replied: “This is the Messenger of Allah ﷺ with the Muhajirun and Ansar.”

Abu Sufyan’s response captured his shock: “These people have never seen power or might before. How did they reach this level?” He paused, then said: “O Abbas, by Allah, today your nephew’s kingdom has become huge.”

Abbas corrected him immediately: “This isn’t a kingdom. This is prophethood.”

Abu Sufyan, still thinking politically, said: “Yes, then it is that. It is that.”

Only at that moment was his pride truly broken. He finally understood: this wasn’t about kingdoms or political power. This was divine mission, prophetic calling in the hands of Allah ﷻ.

The Cycle Complete

This glimpse into the victory of Makkah helps us appreciate what إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ truly meant. These weren’t just abstract concepts - they were lived realities that the companions experienced. The promise had always been there, carried through years of persecution, exile, hunger, and near-annihilation.

The ayah uses إِذَا (when), which in Arabic can push meaning toward the future. So when the Sahaba were being tortured, when they were starving, when they were about to be killed - not even two years before Makkah’s conquest - this promise remained: “When the victory comes, when the aid from Allah comes.” Allah ﷻ had declared: لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي - “I and My messengers will surely prevail.” His promise was always meant to be fulfilled: وَعْدَ اللَّهِ - “the promise of Allah.”

Ayah 2

وَرَأَيْتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًا

“And you see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes.”

The Language of Entry

The simple translation would be: “You’ll see people accept Islam.” But the Quran doesn’t use simple language. There’s a principle in Arabic rhetoric that the best speech is concise - getting the point across efficiently. If Allah ﷻ wanted to simply say people were accepting Islam, He could have used the verb أَسْلَمُوا (they became Muslim). Instead, He uses this prolonged phrase: وَرَأَيْتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًا. What is the advantage of this more elaborate expression?

Connection to the Previous Surah

First, the word دِينِ (religion) creates a direct continuation from Surah Al-Kafirun, where the Prophet ﷺ told the disbelievers: لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ - “For you is your religion, and for me is my religion.” This is crucial because in that previous surah, when the Prophet ﷺ said “my religion,” it appeared he stood alone - all of them had their religions, and only he had his.

But now Allah ﷻ clarifies: when the Messenger says “my religion,” it’s actually Allah’s ﷻ religion. And when you thought in the previous surah that he was alone because all of you have yours and only he has his, look what happens when I give him aid (nasr) - multitudes come into this same religion. This is a profound elaboration of what دِينِي meant. It wasn’t just his religion; it was Allah’s ﷻ religion, and that’s why it would have victory.

The Reality of Following Trends

The second profound insight here addresses a fundamental aspect of human nature: we have always been followers of trends. We see this more clearly now than ever before, but it’s always been true. When most people do things a certain way, your family and you are inclined to follow. Everybody goes to prom - why can’t I? Everybody else is having New Year’s parties - why can’t we? Everyone got those sneakers - why can’t I? Everyone got married in a fancy hall - why do I have to do it in the masjid?

When people establish certain patterns - how they look, talk, behave, even which religious practices they follow - you’re pulled to conform. Our parents did it this way, we went to this place, we did these things. What’s wrong with it? Why should we do anything differently? Trends are an incredibly powerful force in human society.

During this entire period in the Arabian Peninsula, the Quraysh were the trendsetters. Since the Year of the Elephant when Abraha’s army was destroyed, a mythology had developed around them: the Quraysh were so protected by Allah ﷻ that even elephants couldn’t defeat them. They were the custodians of the Kaaba, therefore divinely protected, therefore holy. You don’t mess with them because you don’t want to provoke Allah ﷻ - He destroyed elephants, He can destroy you.

Now that the Quraysh had been defeated, a new mythology emerged among Arabs who didn’t fully understand Islam: “The right religion must be the one with victory. If it achieved victory, it must be the right one.”

But is that actually the correct formula? Because it achieved victory, it must be the right religion? If we followed that logic, Islam would have been right at Badr but wrong at Uhud. This is actually the flawed thinking of the Quraysh themselves. Remember how they said after Uhud: لَنَا الْعُزَّىٰ وَلَا عُزَّىٰ لَكُمْ - “We have al-Uzza and you have no Uzza”? They believed their victory proved their gods were real.

Allah ﷻ corrected this thinking: تِلْكَ الْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ - “These are days We alternate between people.” Victory and loss alternate. The real test happens through these alternations - وَلِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا - “And that Allah may make evident those who truly believe.” When is your belief really tested? Not when you’re winning, but when you’re losing. وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَاءَ - “And that He may take martyrs from among you.” وَاللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الظَّالِمِينَ - “And Allah does not love the wrongdoers.” He doesn’t give them victory because He loves them.

The Danger of Trend-Based Faith

But in most people’s minds, if someone is winning, they must be doing something right. Allah ﷻ must favor them. This thinking extends to individuals: “This person is doing well financially, their health is good, their wealth is good. Allah clearly loves them. They’re not even that religious, so that means it’s okay to be like them. If Allah was upset with them, He wouldn’t bless them like this.”

This is the same disease that afflicted the owner of the two gardens in Surah Al-Kahf: لَئِن رُّدِدتُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهَا مُنقَلَبًا - “If I’m returned to my Lord, He’ll give me something even better than this.” This false equation - worldly success equals divine favor - was the misguided thinking of most Arabs.

Now, because of the Prophet’s ﷺ victory, large numbers began entering Islam. This wasn’t necessarily the deep, conviction-based faith of the early Muslims. The ayah doesn’t say أَسْلَمُوا (they believed) or آمَنُوا (they had faith). It says يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ - “they enter into Allah’s religion.” They officially took the fold of the religion, but nothing more has necessarily happened. They’ve formally joined, but the Prophet ﷺ is being told: you’re not in charge of their hearts. Your job is done now.

This is directly related to Surah Al-Fath, where Allah ﷻ says: وَأُخْرَىٰ لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْهَا - “And other victories you haven’t yet acquired.” The chain of nasr and fath - aid leading to victory, which creates new challenges requiring new aid, which leads to new victories - has now come to completion for the Prophet ﷺ personally. When this ayah says people are entering the religion in multitudes, it marks the fulfillment of that cycle.

The Historical Reality

Remember, Surah Al-Fath came after Hudaybiyyah. By that time, the process of large delegations coming and accepting Islam had already started. After the conquest of Makkah, this process accelerated dramatically. The trend of struggling to convince people one by one in Makkah, where most rejected and mocked the message, had fundamentally changed since Hudaybiyyah.

Now people were coming on their own saying, “We want to become Muslim. Is there some benefit if we do?” The Quran even addresses this, telling the Prophet ﷺ that some were acting like they were doing him a favor by accepting Islam.

The Shift from Resistance to Power

This shift is extremely important to understand. Before Hudaybiyyah, Islam was a constant resistance movement. Muslims were resisting against the dominant power. They were the persecuted minority fighting for survival. Hudaybiyyah marked the transition where Muslims were no longer the resistance minority - they were becoming the dominant power.

When you’re a resistance movement, anyone who joins is a member of the resistance. This means they no longer have aspirations for nice houses or comfortable lives. Their life, their mission, their cause, their children, their spouse - everything is secondary. The resistance comes first. Everything else is second.

But when Islam is in a position of power, it’s no longer a resistance movement. It’s now the established religion of Allah ﷻ. The former powers trying to dominate are gone. You’re now a civilization, not a resistance movement. When you’re a new civilization, you’ll have different categories of people.

You’ll have people who joined Islam as part of the resistance movement and can never see it any other way - for them, Islam is inherently a struggle. But new members will say, “This is a good religion. It makes sense. There’s one God. The Quran is convincing.” They’re not joining a resistance; they’re accepting a religion’s truth and principles. They’ll ask, “What do I have to do to become Muslim?” You tell them: follow the five pillars, obey the basic commandments. If you do this, you’re a good Muslim.

You don’t tell them, “Welcome to the revolution!” because they haven’t joined a revolution. They’ve joined Islam. They haven’t joined the jihad (struggle) of iman - they’ve accepted the religion. If you just obey Allah ﷻ and His Messenger through the five pillars and basic commandments, you’re Muslim.

But إِيمَان - that deeper faith, that willingness to sacrifice everything - that’s for those who carry the original spirit. After Hudaybiyyah, there were now two categories: the general Muslims (عَامَّةُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ) and the smaller group who maintained that intense commitment (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا - those who truly believed).

The Prophet’s Guidance for the New Era

Look at what the Prophet ﷺ did when he sent companions to different towns after this shift. Did he say, “Go invite them to join the jihad against the Quraysh”? No. He said: يَسِّرُوا وَلَا تُعَسِّرُوا - “Make things easy, don’t make things hard.” Were they going there to give people a movement or a struggle? No, they were giving them Islam.

Before Hudaybiyyah, Islam meant asking, “What more can I do?” Anyone who came in didn’t ask what’s the minimum - they asked what’s the maximum they could sacrifice. After Hudaybiyyah, you tell people: “Here’s the least you’re supposed to do. Just do at least this much.”

As teachers of religion, as those who call others to Islam, our job is to spread Islam - to tell people the minimum they need to do to live a good life as Muslims. Some people will be inspired to do more. They won’t sit still. They want to serve the religion more, go out of their way, struggle more. They’ll take inspiration from which generation? The Sahaba. And they’ll find those guidelines in Allah’s ﷻ book.

The Danger of Imposing Impossible Standards

This distinction is crucial. When you talk to the average Muslim and say, “Uthman (RA) gave half of everything he owned, Abu Bakr (RA) gave everything - and you’re just giving Zakat? Are you going to hell?” No, they’re not! There were plenty of companions who didn’t give half or all. The level of sacrifice some made was for that movement, for that cause. We inspire others with their example to do more, but we hold to the Prophet’s ﷺ principle: our job for the vast majority of Muslims is to make things easy.

Then the question arises: if this is the case, how will anyone ever rise to the level of iman? How will they develop that deeper faith? The answer is simple: this is why Allah ﷻ gave us the Quran. وَذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ الذِّكْرَىٰ تَنفَعُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ - “Remind, for indeed the reminder benefits the believers.”

The reminder of the Quran, the inspiration from the Quran, the wisdom (hikmah) of the Quran, the divine counsel (maw’idhah) of the Quran - the more you love the Quran, what does it do? It lights the fire of iman inside you. It makes you want to do more. It brings the struggle of the companions to life. You can’t help but feel: “I can’t believe I’m part of that legacy. It’s the same train - they’re in a cart way ahead, I’m in a cart way back, but we’re still on the same train. I’m part of the same legacy. I’m connected to these people, and that connection is a bigger part of my identity than anything else.”

When you understand and internalize that connection, you naturally want to serve Allah ﷻ in some way. You want to do something more. But that has to come from within. It can’t be imposed from outside. It has to emanate from yourself.

Entering Allah’s Religion

The word أَفْوَاجًا means “in multitudes” or “in large groups.” A فَوْج is a large group, and أَفْوَاجًا indicates multiple such groups - wave after wave of people. The imagery is of masses of humanity flowing into Islam, not individuals converting one by one after long struggles as had been the case in Makkah.

The phrase يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ is significant. Entering suggests going from outside to inside, crossing a threshold. These people are formally joining the Muslim community, taking the shahada, becoming part of the ummah. But the ayah doesn’t describe the state of their hearts or the depth of their conviction. It describes the visible, outward reality: you will see this phenomenon happening.

When people join because of trends - because Islam has become powerful, because it seems like the winning side - they will also leave when trends change. This is exactly what happened after the Prophet’s ﷺ passing. When rumors spread that Islam might not remain as strong without him, large numbers of people left Islam during the Riddah (apostasy) period. Those who had joined because of trend left for the same reason.

This is why the Islam of the companions, the Islam of the original generation, was so different. For the vast majority of them, Islam was against every trend. They had to go against their families, their tribes, their economic interests, their social standing. They had to think for themselves, follow their own conscience, and come to Islam despite every worldly pressure to reject it.

The Modern Application

Today, a dangerous phenomenon emerges: people want Islam itself to follow societal trends. There are existing trends in society, and people want Islam to be molded so it fits those trends, making it more “palatable” for modern audiences. But this approach opens doors for large numbers who will leave just as easily as they came.

True commitment to Islam must come from conviction, not convenience. The Quran’s reminder is what builds that conviction. When someone connects deeply with the Quran, when they understand the legacy they’re part of, when they feel the same faith that moved the Sahaba - that’s when real iman develops. And that comes from genuine engagement with Allah’s ﷻ revelation, not from making Islam trendy or easy to the point where it loses its transformative power.

The ayah describes a historical reality that the Prophet ﷺ would witness: masses of people entering Islam after its victory. But embedded in this description is a profound warning about the difference between those who enter Islam with deep conviction and those who enter following trends, and the responsibility of teachers to maintain both authenticity and accessibility in presenting this religion.

Ayah 3

فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابًۢا

“Then glorify with praise of your Lord and seek His forgiveness. Indeed, He is ever the Accepter of Repentance.”

Ayah 1–3 · linked video: 1. An-Nasr

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ

نَصْرُ (nasru) [ن-ص-ر]

The root ن-ص-ر originally referred to the pathways by which water comes into a valley. Water that makes its way from the mountaintops down to the bottom creates a reservoir from which people can draw help. This imagery conveys the meaning of aid arriving just in time, like life-giving rain to dying earth.

The word نَصْرُ (nasr) means not just ordinary help but great, decisive aid - the kind that arrives at the moment of life or death. It carries the meaning of rescue when one is on the verge of destruction. In the Quran, this term is specifically used for help that comes at the critical moment, distinguishing it from other words for assistance like مَعُونَة (ma’una) or مَدَد (madad).

The construct إِذَا جَاءَ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ uses the definite form, indicating this is THE aid of Allah ﷻ - a specific, promised, divine intervention that has been anticipated throughout the prophetic mission.

الْفَتْحُ (al-fath) [ف-ت-ح]

The root ف-ت-ح literally means to open. It appears in the Quran both literally (فَتَحْنَا أَبْوَابَ السَّمَاءِ - “We opened the gates of the sky” (54:11)) and figuratively. The figurative usage includes separation of two things, opening of blessings, and removal of difficulties.

Historically, when ancient cities had locked gates for security, “opening the city” meant the city had been conquered. This military terminology derives from the imagery of gates being forced open, allowing conquest.

Interestingly, فَتْح is also used in the Quran for a verdict or decision (يَوْمَ الْفَتْحِ - “the Day of Decision”). The idea of separating truth from falsehood, making a final judgment between opposing parties, connects to this root meaning. In the context of Makkah, it means the final separation between the false religion and the true Dean, the decisive opening that would clarify which faith belongs at the House of Allah built by Ibrahim (AS).

The sequence نَصْرُ first, then فَتْح second, is logically consistent: first comes the aid, then as a result of that aid comes the victory or opening. This ordering is significant when compared to Surah Al-Fath, where the sequence is reversed (إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُبِينًا… لِيُنصِرَكَ اللَّهُ نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا) (48:1,3), teaching that Allah ﷻ gives victory first at Hudaybiyah, then promises mighty aid to follow.

Ayah 2

وَرَأَيْتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًۭا

النَّاسَ (an-nasa) [ن-و-س / ن-ي-س]

The definite article with النَّاسَ indicates specific people - not humanity in general, but the people of that time and place who would witness the fulfillment of Allah’s promise. The emphasis is on observable human response to divine aid and victory.

يَدْخُلُونَ (yadkhuluna) [د-خ-ل]

The verb دَخَلَ (dakhala) means to enter. The present/future tense يَدْخُلُونَ with the elongated وا ending emphasizes the ongoing, continuous nature of this entry. The choice of “entering” rather than “believing” (آمَنُوا) (aamanu) or “accepting Islam” (أَسْلَمُوا) (aslamu) is deliberate and significant.

“Entering” suggests crossing a threshold, making an official transition, but doesn’t necessarily indicate depth of conviction. They have entered the formal boundaries of the religion, but their hearts are not explicitly mentioned. This distinguishes those who join Islam as a movement from those who carry it as a cause in their hearts.

The elongated form also creates a visual and auditory effect - you can see and hear the masses coming, wave after wave, which ties directly to the next word.

دِينِ (dini) [د-ي-ن]

The word دِين (deen) here is a direct continuation from the previous surah (Al-Kafirun), where the Prophet ﷺ was told: “You have your deen, and I have mine” (لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ) (109:6). That surah emphasized the Prophet’s ﷺ isolation - “you all have yours (plural), and I alone have mine (singular).”

Now Allah ﷻ clarifies that what appeared to be the Prophet’s ﷺ solitary deen is actually Allah’s Deen (دِينِ اللَّهِ). The possessive construct (إِضَافَة) shifts ownership from the Prophet ﷺ to Allah, elevating its status. When it’s Allah’s Deen, it will inevitably have victory because it’s backed by divine power, not human effort alone.

The term دِين encompasses religion, way of life, judgment, and system - it’s comprehensive. This distinguishes Islam from being merely a set of beliefs; it’s a complete civilization.

أَفْوَاجًا (afwajan) [ف-و-ج]

The root ف-و-ج means a large group or crowd. The plural form أَفْوَاجًا (afwajan) means multiple large groups - groups upon groups upon groups. This conveys not just quantity but the manner of coming: in successive waves, in organized delegations, in tribal units.

The use of a plural of a plural (جمع الجمع) is a rhetorical device emphasizing vast numbers. It’s not individuals trickling in one by one; it’s entire communities, tribes, and delegations arriving together.

This word choice is also significant because it describes a sociological phenomenon. When Islam was a resistance movement, individuals joined despite their tribes. Now that Islam is the dominant power, entire tribes are joining together because of the trend, because their leaders have decided, because it’s the new normal. This crowd mentality is both a blessing (numbers) and a potential concern (shallow commitment).

Ayah 3

فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابًۢا

سَبِّحْ (sabbih) [س-ب-ح]

The root س-ب-ح means to swim or to move freely through space. When applied to Allah , تَسْبِيح means to declare His perfection, to negate any deficiency from Him, to affirm He is free from all imperfections. The image is of something pure moving freely without obstruction or contamination.

The command form فَسَبِّحْ (fasabbih) is emphatic with the ف prefix, meaning “then” or “as a result” - linking this command directly to what came before. Once you see the aid, the victory, and the masses entering, THEN engage in intensive glorification.

The حْ ending without a pronoun object leaves it open, general, continuous - keep doing it, make it your state.

بِحَمْدِ (bi-hamdi) [ح-م-د]

The root ح-م-د means to praise someone for their praiseworthy qualities. The بِ prefix indicates the instrument or means - declare Allah’s perfection BY praising Him, THROUGH praise. This connects تَسْبِيح (negating imperfection) with حَمْد (affirming perfection).

The construct بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ means the glorification must be accompanied by, must be expressed through, praise. You can’t just say “Allah is free from imperfection” abstractly; you must actively enumerate His praiseworthy attributes. This is why we say سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ وَبِحَمْدِهِ - glory to Allah AND with His praise.

رَبِّكَ (rabbika) [ر-ب-ب]

The word رَبّ means Master, Nurturer, Sustainer, Lord - the One who brings things to completion and perfection through stages. The possessive suffix كَ (“your”) personalizes this relationship. Even though masses are entering His Deen, Allah ﷻ addresses the Prophet ﷺ with YOUR Rabb - maintaining the intimate, personal connection.

This is crucial: despite the victory, despite the multitudes, despite the apparent culmination of the mission, the Prophet’s ﷺ relationship with his Rabb remains personal and direct. He’s not told to celebrate with the masses or to glory in the achievement; he’s told to return to his Rabb in intimate worship.

اسْتَغْفِرْهُ (istaghfirhu) [غ-ف-ر]

The root غ-ف-ر means to cover, conceal, or forgive. The استفعال pattern (Form X) indicates seeking or requesting. The attached pronoun هُ makes it direct and personal: seek HIS forgiveness, from HIM specifically.

The command to seek forgiveness immediately after victory is profound. When someone achieves great success, the natural human response is pride, celebration, a sense of accomplishment. Allah ﷻ redirects the Prophet ﷺ to humility and recognition that whatever was achieved was by Allah’s grace, not personal merit.

Seeking forgiveness is also a recognition that even in the best of deeds, there may be shortcomings, moments of self-awareness rather than Allah-awareness, subtle ego involvement. The greatest servant of Allah ﷺ is taught to be most vigilant about these subtle imperfections precisely at the moment of greatest apparent success.

تَوَّابًا (tawwaban) [ت-و-ب]

The root ت-و-ب means to return. The فَعَّال pattern (intensive form) indicates one who does something repeatedly, abundantly, or as a characteristic. تَوَّاب means the One who repeatedly accepts repentance, who is always turning back to His servants with mercy when they turn to Him.

The phrase إِنَّهُ كَانَ تَوَّابًا uses كَانَ (kana) which indicates a permanent, established characteristic - He has ALWAYS been this way, this is His eternal nature, not a temporary mood. The إِنَّ (inna) at the beginning provides emphasis: “Indeed, certainly, truly.”

This ending reassures the Prophet ﷺ and the believers: no matter what has happened, no matter what stage the mission has reached, Allah’s door of repentance remains open. Even if you think you’ve done everything right, come back to Him. He’s always been the One who accepts those who return.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 1

إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ

The conditional particle إِذَا (idha) is crucial to understand. Unlike إِنْ (in), which expresses a hypothetical condition, إِذَا expresses a condition that is certain to occur. The structure إِذَا جَاءَ doesn’t mean “IF the help comes” but rather “WHEN the help comes” - it’s guaranteed, it’s a divine promise.

There’s a scholarly debate about whether this should be understood in past tense or future tense. The perfect verb جَاءَ typically indicates past tense, but when it follows إِذَا, it can push the meaning into the future - describing something so certain it’s spoken of as if it already happened. Some scholars translate it as “When the help of Allah has arrived” (past), others as “When the help of Allah arrives” (future).

The argument for the Makkan revelation interpretation rests partly on this grammatical ambiguity: if revealed in Makkah, it’s clearly future (prophetic promise); if revealed in Madinah or at the conquest, it could be past or present perfect (fulfilled promise).

The coupling وَالْفَتْحُ (wal-fath) uses وَ (wa) which is typically translated as “and,” but in Arabic rhetoric, وَ can indicate consequence or result. So this could mean “and as a result, the opening” or “along with the opening.” The sequence matters: نَصْر comes first (means to the goal), then فَتْح (the goal itself).

The construct إضافة (possessive/genitive construction) in نَصْرُ اللَّهِ (nasr-Allah) is significant. It could mean “the help FROM Allah” (source), “the help OF Allah” (possession), or “the help GIVEN BY Allah” (agent). All three meanings are intended - this is the comprehensive, divine aid that only Allah ﷻ can provide.

Ayah 2

وَرَأَيْتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًۭا

The verb وَرَأَيْتَ (wa ra’ayta) - “and you will see” - is visual, observable, empirical. This isn’t about spiritual insight or divine revelation; it’s about physical witness. The Prophet ﷺ will SEE with his own eyes the masses coming. This grounds the surah in historical, observable reality.

The structure رَأَيْتَ النَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ creates a present progressive or continuous aspect: “you will see the people [in the act of] entering.” The verb يَدْخُلُونَ is a مضارع (present/future) verb functioning as a حال (circumstantial) - describing the state or action as it’s happening.

The phrase دِينِ اللَّهِ (deenillah) is another إضافة construct, and notice that دِين is indefinite in construct but becomes definite through its attachment to اللَّه. It’s not “a religion” but THE Deen, specifically Allah’s Deen.

The use of أَفْوَاجًا as a حال منصوب (accusative circumstantial noun) is eloquent. It could have said يَدْخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ بِأَفْوَاجٍ (entering into Allah’s Deen in groups), but instead, it says يَدْخُلُونَ… أَفْوَاجًا. The ellipsis (حذف) and rearrangement create a more powerful image - the groups are so prominent, so overwhelming, that they become the defining characteristic of the scene.

Rhetorically, the phrase فِي دِينِ اللَّهِ (into Allah’s Deen) is positioned between the verb and its manner (أَفْوَاجًا), creating a sandwich structure that emphasizes the Deen itself. The masses are entering, but what matters is WHAT they’re entering.

Ayah 3

فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابًۢا

The فَ prefix on فَسَبِّحْ (fa sabbih) is فاء الفصيحة (fa’ of deduction/consequence), indicating “then,” “therefore,” “as a result.” This connects the command to what preceded: BECAUSE the help came, BECAUSE the victory arrived, BECAUSE the masses are entering, THEREFORE engage in tasbih.

The command فَسَبِّحْ (imperative) followed by بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ (bi hamdi rabbik) creates a beautiful rhetorical structure. The بِ prefix indicates instrumentality or accompaniment - the tasbih must be done BY MEANS OF or ACCOMPANIED BY praise. This prevents empty glorification; it must be substantive, filled with actual praise.

The word order رَبِّكَ (rabbaka) before the verb اسْتَغْفِرْهُ (istaghfirhu) is significant. In Arabic, the typical order would be اسْتَغْفِرْ رَبَّكَ (seek forgiveness from your Rabb), but here رَبَّكَ is advanced (تقديم) for emphasis. Before you’re even told to seek forgiveness, you’re reminded: He is YOUR Rabb, your personal Master and Nurturer.

The separation of the two commands - سَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَاسْتَغْفِرْهُ - with the وَ creates a pause, a breath. Do this (glorify with praise), AND do this (seek forgiveness). They’re distinct acts but linked, sequential but interconnected.

The concluding clause إِنَّهُ كَانَ تَوَّابًا uses إِنَّ (inna) for emphasis (“indeed”), كَانَ (kaana) to indicate a permanent characteristic (“He has always been”), and تَوَّابًا (tawwaba) (the intensive form) to indicate abundance and repetition. The rhetorical effect is layered reassurance: not only is He forgiving, He’s ALWAYS been forgiving, REPEATEDLY forgiving, ABUNDANTLY forgiving.

The choice of تَوَّاب (tawwab) over غَفُور (ghafoor) (another name meaning Forgiving) is rhetorically significant. غَفُور emphasizes the ACT of forgiving, while تَوَّاب emphasizes the RELATIONSHIP - He turns toward those who turn toward Him. It’s relational, reciprocal, dynamic. This fits the intimate tone of رَبِّكَ - your personal Rabb who turns toward you when you turn toward Him.

The entire surah uses extremely concise language. Three short ayahs, minimal words, yet conveying profound, multilayered meanings. This is إيجاز (conciseness), a hallmark of Quranic rhetoric. Every word counts; nothing is superfluous. The brevity itself communicates urgency and finality.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 1

إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ

The historical context of this surah’s revelation is debated. The majority of scholars say it was revealed in Madinah, with some specifying it came on the day Makkah was conquered, others saying it was revealed on the way to Makkah’s conquest, and still others saying it was revealed while the Prophet ﷺ was residing somewhere during that journey.

A minority position holds that this is a Makkan surah. The argument for Makkan revelation is that this was a promise being given when the Prophet ﷺ was still in Makkah, still facing persecution, still in the resistance phase. The future-oriented language of إِذَا جَاءَ supports this interpretation.

A famous narration connects this surah to the Prophet’s passing. Ibn Abbas (RA) reported that Umar (RA) would quiz the senior companions about the meaning of this surah after the Prophet’s ﷺ death. They would offer various interpretations, but Umar (RA) would reject them. When he asked Ibn Abbas (RA) - who was much younger - Ibn Abbas (RA) said this was the farewell address of the Prophet ﷺ, indicating his time to leave this world had come. Umar (RA) confirmed that Ibn Abbas (RA) had understood correctly.

From this narration, it was understood that when this surah came, it was Allah’s way of saying goodbye to His Messenger ﷺ, informing him that his mission was complete. However, this doesn’t necessarily prove when it was revealed initially - only that it gained this meaning by the time of the conquest and the Prophet’s final days.

The relationship between this surah and Surah Al-Fath (48) is crucial historically. Surah Al-Fath was revealed on the return journey from Hudaybiyah. Both surahs use the same key terms: نَصْر and فَتْح. Surah Al-Fath calls Hudaybiyah “an opening, a clear victory” (فَتْحًا مُبِينًا), which confused the companions because they saw it as a humiliating retreat, not a victory.

Hudaybiyah: The Historical Context

Hudaybiyah occurred roughly a year or less after the Battle of the Trench (Al-Ahzab), when Quraysh and allied tribes had besieged Madinah, attempting to annihilate every Muslim. The Muslims were saved only by digging a defensive trench and by divine intervention through winds that disrupted the enemy coalition.

After this close call, the Prophet ﷺ saw a dream that the Muslims would perform Hajj. Acting on this vision, he took approximately 1,400-1,800 companions toward Makkah, unarmed except for small daggers for animal sacrifice. They brought sacrificial animals, which slowed their travel considerably.

When Quraysh learned the Muslims were approaching, they planned an ambush. The Muslims took an alternate, extremely difficult path to avoid the attack. The companions’ feet were bloodied from the rocky, thorny terrain. They arrived at Hudaybiyah, a location very close to Makkah (described as “from Long Island to Queens” in the lecture - meaning within sight of the city).

Quraysh sent 80 cloaked assassins to kill the Muslims quietly at Hudaybiyah. The Muslims captured all 80, disarmed them, exposed their Qurayshi identity, and then released them. This demonstrated both strength and restraint.

The Muslims’ blood was boiling by this point. They had been attacked repeatedly, driven from their homes, and now their enemies had tried to kill them in holy land where killing is forbidden even by Quraysh’s own pre-Islamic customs. Abu Bakr (RA) calmed the crowd by reminding them they came for Hajj because of the Prophet’s ﷺ dream, not for war.

Negotiations ensued. The climax came when Abu Jandal (RA), the son of Quraysh’s negotiator, escaped from captivity in Makkah where his own father had imprisoned and tortured him for becoming Muslim. He arrived in chains, bloodied, pleading for sanctuary. His father demanded his return as part of the treaty terms: any Muslim who escapes from Makkah must be returned, but anyone who leaves Islam and flees to Makkah may stay. The terms were manifestly unfair.

Additionally, the treaty stipulated that Muslims could NOT perform Hajj that year but could return the following year. The Muslims were to slaughter their sacrificial animals right there and give the meat to the very people who had tried to kill them.

During negotiations, a rumor spread that Uthman (RA), who had gone into Makkah to negotiate, had been killed. This news inflamed the Muslims to the point that the Prophet ﷺ himself - who had been counseling restraint - took a pledge from the companions to fight to the death. This is the famous Bay’ah al-Ridwan, recorded in Surah Al-Fath (48:18): “Indeed, Allah was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree.”

When Uthman (RA) returned safely, the Prophet ﷺ agreed to the treaty terms despite their apparent unfairness. He commanded the companions to shave their heads and slaughter their animals, but for the first time in Islamic history, the companions did not immediately obey. They stood frozen in disbelief and anger.

Abu Bakr (RA) and Umar (RA) - typically models of obedience - were shaken. Umar (RA) approached Abu Bakr (RA) and questioned whether the Prophet ﷺ was truly Allah’s messenger, whether they were truly on the right path, why they were humiliating themselves by accepting these terms, and whether the dream of performing Hajj had been false since they weren’t performing it “this year.”

Abu Bakr (RA) told Umar (RA) to watch himself, but even Abu Bakr (RA) did not immediately shave his head or slaughter his animal. Only after the Prophet’s ﷺ wife Umm Salamah (RA) advised him to perform the acts himself without speaking did the companions begrudgingly follow suit.

As they returned to Madinah, Umar (RA) - feeling guilty about raising his voice to the Prophet ﷺ (a sin that could nullify all one’s good deeds per Surah Al-Hujurat 49:2) - tried three times to approach the Prophet ﷺ, who ignored him each time. Umar (RA) thought he was doomed.

Then the Prophet ﷺ called for Umar (RA). Umar (RA) approached nervously, and the Prophet’s ﷺ face was “brighter than the sun” with joy. He recited the entire Surah Al-Fath (all four pages), which begins: “Indeed, We have given you a CLEAR victory.”

When the Prophet ﷺ finished, Umar (RA) asked in disbelief: “Is THAT a victory?” The Prophet ﷺ emphatically responded: “YES, it is.”

Why Hudaybiyah Was Victory

The victory of Hudaybiyah wasn’t military conquest or territorial gain. It was the DISCIPLINE of the companions under the most extreme, seemingly unjust circumstances. Over 1,400-1,800 men, many battle-hardened, all with legitimate grievances, all witnessing their Prophet ﷺ apparently humiliated and their brother Abu Jandal sent back to torture - and they maintained discipline.

Their only “failure” was delaying obeying the command to shave their heads. But no riot occurred. No mutiny. No breaking ranks. They stayed with their Prophet ﷺ even when his decisions made no sense to them.

This is the victory Allah ﷻ celebrates in Surah Al-Fath: “Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah, and those with him are fierce against the disbelievers, merciful among themselves… like a seed that puts forth its shoot, then strengthens it, then it becomes thick and stands upon its stem, delighting the sowers” (48:29). If there was ever a moment when they seemed NOT to be “with him,” it was Hudaybiyah - yet Allah ﷻ affirmed they WERE with him. That loyalty under impossible conditions qualified them for Allah’s aid (نَصْر).

Politically, Hudaybiyah was genius. Quraysh recognized the Muslims as a legitimate power by negotiating with them as equals. The treaty restricted Quraysh’s ability to form alliances against the Muslims. Neutral tribes saw that the Muslims had approached Quraysh unarmed, and Quraysh had been too afraid to let them enter, choosing negotiation instead. This made the Muslims appear stronger and Quraysh weaker.

The two-year period before the conquest of Makkah saw an explosion in conversions. Entire tribes sent delegations to investigate Islam, and many accepted. When a tribal leader became Muslim, often his entire tribe followed. Islam’s influence expanded dramatically while Quraysh’s influence shrank.

When Quraysh eventually broke the treaty by supporting an attack on a tribe allied with the Muslims, the political table had completely turned. Abu Sufyan (RA), the leader of Quraysh, came to Madinah to plead for the treaty to remain in effect, but no one would give him an audience. The Prophet ﷺ had no interest in renewing a treaty Quraysh had broken.

The Conquest of Makkah

The conquest occurred approximately two years after Hudaybiyah. The Prophet ﷺ marched toward Makkah with 10,000 men - a massive force compared to previous battles. He commanded each group to light a campfire, so 10,000 fires illuminated the desert night on Makkah’s borders - a psychological display of overwhelming power.

Abbas (RA), the Prophet’s ﷺ uncle, borrowed the Prophet’s ﷺ mule and rode into Makkah’s outskirts to encourage any Qurayshi leaders to come and seek protection before the Muslims entered aggressively. He encountered Abu Sufyan (RA) and other leaders, who were terrified when they saw the fires.

Abbas (RA) recognized their voices in the darkness and called out to Abu Sufyan (RA) by his nickname. After confirming identities, Abu Sufyan (RA) asked, “What’s happening?” Abbas (RA) responded: “This is the Messenger of Allah with all the peoples (multiple tribes). What a morning is about to hit Quraysh! If the Prophet captures you, he will cut your neck off. Get on the back of my mule, and I’ll take you to him so you can seek his protection. That’s your only way out.”

Abu Sufyan (RA) agreed and rode behind Abbas (RA) on the Prophet’s ﷺ mule through the Muslim camp. Every time they passed a fire, Muslims would ask, “Who is this?” - the same question Abu Sufyan (RA) used to ask dismissively about others. When they recognized the Prophet’s mule and Abbas (RA), they let them pass.

When they reached Umar’s (RA) encampment, Umar (RA) recognized Abu Sufyan (RA) - the man responsible for countless Muslim deaths and suffering - and asked for permission to kill him, saying, “There’s no contract or treaty now. Can I take his head off?” Abbas (RA) insisted he had promised safety.

Umar (RA) accused Abbas (RA) of being biased because Abu Sufyan (RA) was from Abbas’s (RA) tribe. Abbas (RA) responded: “I swear, the day you became Muslim was dearer to me than even if my own father had become Muslim. Your Islam is more beloved to me than my own father’s Islam would have been, because your Islam is dear to the Prophet ﷺ. Don’t accuse me of tribalism.”

The Prophet ﷺ told them to take Abu Sufyan (RA) away and deal with him in the morning. In the morning, the Prophet ﷺ asked Abu Sufyan (RA) : “Isn’t it time yet that you come to know there is no one worthy of worship except Allah?” Abu Sufyan responded with praises of the Prophet’s ﷺ character and familial loyalty, then said, “If there were any other god besides Allah, he would have helped me by now.”

When the Prophet ﷺ asked, “Isn’t it time for you to acknowledge that I am Allah’s Messenger?” Abu Sufyan praised him again but admitted he still had doubt about that specific claim. Abbas (RA), frustrated with his friend’s stubbornness, told him to stop being foolish and accept Islam before they killed him. Abu Sufyan (RA) then took the shahada and became Muslim.

Abbas (RA) suggested giving Abu Sufyan (RA) some honor to help create a positive ripple effect in Makkah. The Prophet ﷺ declared: “Whoever enters Abu Sufyan’s house is safe. Whoever closes his door is safe. Whoever enters the mosque is safe.” This gave Abu Sufyan (RA) a position of dignity and prevented a potential massacre or riot.

Before Abu Sufyan (RA) left, the Prophet ﷺ told Abbas (RA) to hold him at a narrow point in the valley so he could watch Allah’s armies pass by. Abu Sufyan (RA), a man who had wielded power his entire life, needed to see what true power looked like - power that belonged to Allah ﷻ, not to any human being.

As different tribes’ battalions passed with their flags, Abu Sufyan (RA) kept asking Abbas (RA), “Who is this tribe?” Many were tribes Abu Sufyan (RA) had never dealt with, never even heard of - yet they had traveled to fight on behalf of the Prophet ﷺ. This demonstrated how far Islam’s reach had extended.

Finally, the Prophet’s ﷺ close companions - the Muhajirun and Ansar - passed in complete armor, carrying the green flag. Abu Sufyan (RA) could only see the black shine of their armor; their faces were covered. He asked who they were. Abbas (RA) said, “This is the Messenger of Allah with the Muhajirun and Ansar” - the very people Quraysh had expelled and oppressed.

Abu Sufyan (RA) said, “These people have never seen power or might before. How did they reach this level?” He was acknowledging that the poor, the farmers, the expelled - people with no worldly power - had become an unstoppable force.

Then Abu Sufyan (RA) said to Abbas (RA), “Your nephew’s kingdom has become huge.” He was still thinking in political terms - viewing Islam as a kingdom, the Prophet ﷺ as a king. Abbas (RA) corrected him: “This is not a kingdom. This is prophethood.” Abu Sufyan (RA) replied, “Then it is, then it is” - finally acknowledging the spiritual reality, not just the political one.

This was the moment of Abu Sufyan’s (RA) internal breaking - when he finally let go of tribal pride, political calculation, and worldly thinking, and recognized that what he was witnessing was divine intervention, not human empire-building.

Connection to Ayah 1

إِذَا جَآءَ نَصْرُ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْفَتْحُ

The historical context shows that نَصْرُ اللَّهِ (the aid of Allah) came in stages throughout the Prophet’s ﷺ mission, with each stage of aid leading to a new opening (فَتْح), which then required new aid for the next challenge. This cycle continued until the final, complete opening - the conquest of Makkah, where the idols were destroyed and the Ka’bah was purified.

If this surah was revealed in Makkah (the minority position argued in the lecture), then it was a prophetic promise given during the darkest days of persecution: “When My aid comes, and the opening happens, you will see something remarkable.” The Prophet ﷺ and the early Muslims could hold onto this promise.

If it was revealed in Madinah or during the conquest (the majority position), then it was Allah’s announcement that the cycle had reached its completion: “The final aid has come, the ultimate opening has occurred, now here’s what you must do.”

Either way, the surah connects deeply to the lived experience of the Prophet ﷺ and the companions, who had endured years of persecution, exile, warfare, and uncertainty before witnessing the fulfillment of Allah’s ﷻ promise.

Ayah 2

وَرَأَيْتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدْخُلُونَ فِى دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفْوَاجًۭا

The phrase “you will see the people entering Allah’s Deen in multitudes” has profound historical context related to the shift from Islam as a resistance movement to Islam as an established power.

Pre-Hudaybiyah: Islam as Resistance

Before Hudaybiyah, Islam was a constant resistance movement. Muslims were resisting the dominant power (Quraysh and their allies). Anyone who joined Islam was joining a struggle, a cause, a movement that demanded total sacrifice. Early Muslims like Abu Bakr (RA) gave half their wealth, Bilal (RA) endured torture, the Muhajirun abandoned their homes and property, and everyone understood that Islam came first, everything else second.

The mission was survival and spreading the message against overwhelming opposition. Victory was measured in terms of holding fast to the faith despite persecution, not in terms of territorial control or numbers.

Post-Hudaybiyah: Islam as Established Deen

After Hudaybiyah, Islam transitioned from a resistance movement to a recognized political entity with treaties, diplomatic relations, and growing influence. After the conquest of Makkah, Islam became the dominant power in the Arabian Peninsula.

When Islam is the established religion, the psychology of conversion changes entirely. People are no longer joining a risky underground resistance; they’re joining the winning side, the dominant culture, the new normal.

Tribal leaders would come with their entire tribes and say, “We want to become Muslim.” Some even asked for incentives: “Can we get a discount on zakat?” or “What’s in it for us?” This was unthinkable during the Makkan period.

This explains the specific word choice in the ayah: يَدْخُلُونَ (they are entering) rather than آمَنُوا (they believed) or أَسْلَمُوا (they accepted Islam). “Entering” is a more neutral term - they’re crossing a threshold, making an official transition, but their heart condition isn’t explicitly stated. They’ve entered the formal boundaries of the Deen, but depth of conviction isn’t guaranteed.

The masses entering أَفْوَاجًا (in groups) reflects tribal and crowd mentality. When a leader decides, his followers follow. When it becomes a trend, people join the trend. This is both a blessing (large numbers strengthening the Ummah) and a concern (shallow commitment, potential for mass apostasy if trends shift).

Historical Validation: The Ridda Wars

History validated this concern. When the Prophet ﷺ passed away and there was momentary chaos about succession, large numbers of people apostatized (ridda). Many tribes that had entered Islam “in groups” left Islam in groups. They had joined because it was the winning side, so when they perceived weakness, they left.

Abu Bakr (RA) had to fight the Wars of Apostasy (Ridda Wars) to bring stability. This doesn’t mean everyone who entered in large numbers was insincere, but it demonstrates that mass conversions during a period of power and success carry different dynamics than individual conversions during persecution.

Ayah 3

فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابًۢا

The command to engage in intensive glorification (tasbih) and to seek forgiveness (istighfar) after victory is historically countercultural and psychologically profound.

Normally, after a great victory, conquerors celebrate, boast, take pride in their accomplishments, and solidify their power. The natural human response is, “We did it! We won! We’re powerful!”

Allah ﷻ redirects the Prophet ﷺ away from this response entirely. Instead of celebration, engage in worship. Instead of pride, engage in humility and repentance. Recognize that whatever was achieved was by Allah’s grace, not human merit.

This command also prepares the Prophet ﷺ for his imminent departure from this world. The intensive glorification and seeking forgiveness are the final acts of his prophetic mission - not managing the new Islamic state, not organizing the growing Ummah, but returning to Allah ﷻ in worship.

Historically, the Prophet ﷺ increased his glorification and seeking forgiveness dramatically after this surah was revealed. Companions noticed he would frequently say, “SubhanAllah wa bihamdihi, astaghfirullah wa atubu ilayh” (Glory to Allah and by His praise, I seek Allah’s forgiveness and repent to Him).

When asked about this, he would reference this surah. He understood that his mission was complete, and his time in this world was short. The conquest of Makkah occurred in the 8th year after Hijrah; the Prophet ﷺ passed away in the 11th year - roughly 2-3 years later.

Ibn Abbas’s (RA) interpretation - that this surah was the Prophet’s ﷺ farewell address - is validated by this historical timeline and the Prophet’s ﷺ behavioral changes after its revelation.

No Specific Biblical Context

Unlike many Quranic passages that reference or parallel previous scriptures, this surah does not have direct biblical parallels or references to Israelite history. It’s specifically about the Arabian context, the Makkan-Madinan experience, and the completion of the Prophet Muhammad’s ﷺ mission.

Coherence

Connection to Surah Al-Kafirun (109)

Surah An-Nasr is intimately connected to the surah immediately before it, Surah Al-Kafirun. In Al-Kafirun, the Prophet ﷺ is told to declare to the disbelievers: “You have your deen, and I have my deen” (لَكُمْ دِينُكُمْ وَلِيَ دِينِ) (109:6). The language emphasizes isolation and separation: “You all (plural) have yours, and I alone (singular) have mine.”

At the time of Al-Kafirun’s revelation, Islam appeared to be one man’s solitary religion against the world. But An-Nasr clarifies: what seemed to be the Prophet’s ﷺ personal deen is actually Allah’s Deen (دِينِ اللَّهِ). The possessive shifts from the Prophet ﷺ to Allah ﷻ, elevating its status.

Moreover, where Al-Kafirun emphasized the Prophet’s ﷺ solitude (“I have my deen”), An-Nasr shows the reversal: “You will see the people entering Allah’s Deen in multitudes.” The isolation is over; the separation is about to end; the promise is about to be fulfilled.

The thematic arc from Al-Kafirun to An-Nasr is: from lone resistance to collective victory, from human religion to divine Deen, from rejection to mass acceptance.

Connection to Surah Al-Fath (48)

Both Surah Al-Fath and Surah An-Nasr use the key terms نَصْر (aid) and فَتْح (opening/victory), but the sequence is reversed:

  • Al-Fath (48:1,3): إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُبِينًا… لِيُنصِرَكَ اللَّهُ نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا - “Indeed, We have given you a clear opening… that Allah may aid you with a mighty aid.” Here, فَتْح comes first, then نَصْر follows.

  • An-Nasr (110:1): إِذَا جَاءَ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ وَالْفَتْحُ - “When the aid of Allah comes, and the opening.” Here, نَصْر comes first, then فَتْح follows.

This reversal is meaningful.
